summary.applicability_rationale: RFC 8827 defines the security architecture for WebRTC applications, covering DTLS-SRTP keying, origin-based security, device permissions, communications consent, IP location privacy, and peer identity. While the browser implements the low-level DTLS/SRTP protocol mechanics, the RFC places numerous requirements on the application layer regarding HTTPS usage, signaling security, device permission handling, IP privacy controls, encryption verification UI, and identity provider integration. The web-conference application is directly subject to many of these requirements as the 'calling service' in the RFC's trust model.

findings.requirement: The origin can only be securely established if data is transferred over HTTPS. Clients MUST treat HTTP and HTTPS origins as different permissions domains.

source_files.code_snippet:
const app = express();
const server = http.createServer(app);

services:
  - type: web
    name: web-conference
    runtime: node
    buildCommand: npm install
    startCommand: node server/index.js
    envVars:
      - key: NODE_ENV
        value: production
      - key: OPENAI_API_KEY
        sync: false

evidence.observation: The server uses http.createServer() rather than https.createServer(), meaning it does not natively serve content over HTTPS. However, the render.yaml configuration indicates deployment on Render.com, which provides automatic HTTPS termination at the platform level via a reverse proxy. In production, users would access the application over HTTPS. However, the application itself does not enforce HTTPS, does not redirect HTTP to HTTPS, and in local development runs without TLS. The server does not set HSTS headers or any other mechanism to ensure HTTPS is used.
evidence.gap_description: The application relies entirely on the hosting platform for HTTPS. There is no application-level HTTPS enforcement, no HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect, and no HSTS headers. In non-platform deployments or local development, the application runs over plain HTTP, which violates the origin security model required by RFC 8827.

findings.remediation: Add explicit HTTPS enforcement at the application level: (1) Redirect all HTTP requests to HTTPS when NODE_ENV is 'production', (2) Set the Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header, (3) Consider using https.createServer() with TLS certificates for non-platform deployments.

findings.standard_section: 6.1 Origin and Web Security Issues - Mixed Content
findings.requirement: Browsers which allow active mixed content MUST nevertheless disable WebRTC functionality in mixed content settings. Implementations MUST either choose to terminate the call or display a warning if a page becomes mixed content during a call.

evidence.observation: Mixed content enforcement is a browser-level responsibility. Modern browsers block active mixed content by default and will refuse to load WebRTC APIs on non-secure pages. The application does not load any mixed content (no HTTP resources on an HTTPS page). This is a browser implementation requirement, not an application requirement.

findings.requirement: Implementations MUST obtain explicit user consent prior to providing access to the camera and/or microphone. Implementations MUST at minimum support one-time and permanent camera/microphone access requests for HTTPS origins. Implementations MUST refuse all permissions grants for HTTP origins.

evidence.observation: The application uses navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia() which triggers the browser's built-in device permission dialog. The browser handles consent according to its permission model (one-time vs. permanent). The application gracefully handles the case where video permission is denied by falling back to audio-only. The browser itself enforces the HTTPS-only requirement for getUserMedia.

findings.requirement: Browsers MUST NOT permit permanent screen or application sharing permissions. Browsers MUST provide a separate dialog request for screen/application sharing permissions. The browser MUST indicate any windows which are currently being shared.

evidence.observation: The application uses navigator.mediaDevices.getDisplayMedia() for screen sharing, which triggers the browser's native screen sharing permission dialog. This dialog is separate from the camera/microphone permission dialog. The browser handles all screen sharing permission requirements including the sharing indicator and the prohibition of permanent screen sharing permissions. The application correctly handles the screen sharing lifecycle including the 'ended' event on the screen track.

findings.requirement: The UI MUST clearly indicate when the user's camera and microphone are in use. This indication MUST NOT be suppressible by the JS and MUST clearly indicate how to terminate device access.

evidence.observation: The browser provides its own camera/microphone indicator (typically a red dot or icon in the tab/address bar) that cannot be suppressed by JavaScript. The application additionally provides UI controls (mute/unmute audio, start/stop video) and a 'Leave Room' button that terminates all media access. The application properly stops all tracks in the leaveRoom() method. The browser-level indicators satisfy the MUST requirement; the application-level controls provide additional usability.

findings.requirement: Browser client implementations of WebRTC MUST implement ICE. Browser implementations MUST verify reachability via ICE prior to sending any non-ICE packets. Implementations MUST NOT provide the ICE transaction ID to JavaScript during the lifetime of the transaction. The JS MUST NOT be permitted to control the local ufrag and password.

evidence.observation: ICE is implemented by the browser's WebRTC stack. The application configures ICE servers and passes ICE candidates through the signaling channel. The browser enforces ICE connectivity checks before sending media. The JavaScript code does not attempt to access ICE transaction IDs or control the ufrag/password. The RTCPeerConnection API naturally enforces all these MUST requirements at the browser level. The application does not attempt to circumvent any ICE consent mechanisms.

findings.requirement: WebRTC implementations MUST periodically send keepalives using the consent freshness mechanism specified in RFC 7675. If a keepalive fails and no new ICE channels can be established, then the session MUST be terminated.

evidence.observation: Consent freshness (RFC 7675) is implemented by the browser's ICE stack. The browser automatically sends periodic STUN Binding Requests to maintain consent and will transition the connection state to 'failed' if consent cannot be maintained. The application monitors connection state changes and removes the peer (closing the connection) when the state becomes 'disconnected' or 'failed'. This is appropriate behavior for session termination on consent failure.

findings.requirement: The API MUST provide a mechanism for the calling application JS to indicate that only TURN candidates are to be used. This prevents the peer from learning one's IP address. The API MUST provide a mechanism to suppress ICE negotiation until the user has decided to answer the call.

evidence.observation: While the API requirement is technically a browser API requirement (the RTCPeerConnection API does support iceTransportPolicy: 'relay' to force TURN-only mode), the application bears responsibility for using these mechanisms to protect user privacy. The application does not configure any TURN servers at all, making it impossible to use TURN-only mode for IP privacy. Additionally, ICE negotiation begins immediately when users join the room (upon receiving 'existing-users' or 'user-joined' events) without any opportunity for the callee to accept or decline the call first. This means a user's IP address is exposed to all other participants the moment they join the room, before any explicit consent to communicate with specific peers.
evidence.gap_description: No TURN servers are configured, so TURN-only mode for IP privacy is impossible. ICE negotiation starts automatically on room join without any accept/decline mechanism for incoming connections. Users' IP addresses (host and server-reflexive candidates) are exposed to all peers immediately.

findings.remediation: Configure TURN server(s) and provide a user-facing option to enable TURN-only mode (iceTransportPolicy: 'relay') for users who want to hide their IP address. Consider adding a call accept/decline UI so that ICE negotiation does not begin until the user explicitly agrees to connect with a specific peer.

findings.requirement: Implementations MUST support SRTP, DTLS, and DTLS-SRTP for SRTP keying. Implementations MUST support SCTP over DTLS. All media channels MUST be secured via SRTP/SRTCP. Media traffic MUST NOT be sent over plain RTP/RTCP. Implementations MUST NOT negotiate cipher suites with NULL encryption. DTLS-SRTP MUST be offered for every media channel.

source_files.code_snippet: const connection = new RTCPeerConnection(this.iceServers);

evidence.observation: All of these requirements are enforced by the browser's WebRTC implementation. When RTCPeerConnection is used, the browser automatically uses DTLS-SRTP for all media channels. The browser does not offer plain RTP or NULL encryption cipher suites. The application uses default RTCPeerConnection configuration without any attempts to disable or weaken encryption. WebRTC implementations in all major browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) enforce mandatory DTLS-SRTP and do not support SDP Security Descriptions (SDES) or NULL encryption.

findings.standard_section: 6.5 Communications Security - SDP Security Descriptions and MKI
findings.requirement: WebRTC implementations MUST NOT offer SDP security descriptions (RFC 4568) or select it if offered. An SRTP Master Key Identifier (MKI) MUST NOT be used.

evidence.observation: The application uses the standard RTCPeerConnection createOffer()/createAnswer() methods without any SDP manipulation. The browser's WebRTC implementation does not include SDP Security Descriptions (a]crypto lines) in its SDP and does not use SRTP MKI. The application passes SDP objects through the signaling channel without modification, so it does not inadvertently introduce SDES or MKI.

findings.requirement: All implementations MUST support DTLS 1.2 with TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 cipher suite and the P-256 curve. SRTP_AES128_CM_HMAC_SHA1_80 MUST be supported. Implementations MUST favor cipher suites which support Forward Secrecy. Implementations MUST NOT implement DTLS renegotiation. Endpoints MUST NOT implement TLS False Start.

evidence.observation: These are browser implementation requirements. All major browsers support DTLS 1.2 with the mandatory cipher suite and SRTP protection profile. The application has no control over DTLS cipher suite selection, renegotiation behavior, or TLS False Start. Compliance depends entirely on the browser used by the participants. Assessment would require testing each target browser's WebRTC implementation.

findings.requirement: The API MUST generate a new authentication key pair for every new call by default (for unlinkability). The API MUST provide a means to reuse a key pair for calls. Different key pairs MUST be used for each origin unless specifically configured. When DTLS-SRTP is used, the API MUST NOT permit the JS to obtain the negotiated keying material.

source_files.code_snippet: const connection = new RTCPeerConnection(this.iceServers);

evidence.observation: These requirements relate to the browser's RTCPeerConnection API implementation. The application creates new RTCPeerConnection instances per peer and does not use the certificates option in the RTCConfiguration to reuse key pairs, which means the browser will generate fresh ephemeral keys for each connection (the default behavior per the WebRTC API spec). The application does not attempt to access DTLS keying material. However, verifying that the browser correctly implements ephemeral key generation and per-origin key isolation requires browser-level testing.

findings.requirement: A user-oriented client MUST provide an 'inspector' interface to determine the 'security characteristics' of the media. This MUST include cryptographic algorithms in use, whether Forward Secrecy is provided, a mechanism for out-of-band peer verification (e.g., fingerprint or SAS), and verified identity information for directly verified peers.

source_files.code_snippet: [Full room HTML - no encryption status UI elements present]
[Full room JS - no getStats() calls for encryption verification, no security inspector UI]

evidence.observation: The application does not provide any user-facing security information. There is no encryption indicator (e.g., a lock icon), no way for users to inspect the DTLS cipher suite in use, no indication of whether Forward Secrecy is active, and no mechanism for out-of-band peer verification (such as displaying the DTLS certificate fingerprint or a Short Authentication String). The RTCPeerConnection.getStats() API could be used to retrieve transport stats including dtlsCipher and srtpCipher, but the application does not call this API. While the browser provides some ability to inspect WebRTC internals (e.g., chrome://webrtc-internals), the RFC places this requirement on the 'user-oriented client', which includes the web application.
evidence.gap_description: No security characteristics UI is provided. Users cannot determine what encryption is in use, whether Forward Secrecy is active, or verify peer identity. No DTLS fingerprint comparison or SAS mechanism is available.

findings.remediation: Implement a security inspector UI that: (1) Shows an encryption indicator (lock icon) when DTLS-SRTP is active, (2) Uses RTCPeerConnection.getStats() to retrieve and display the DTLS cipher suite and SRTP protection profile, (3) Displays the remote DTLS certificate fingerprint to allow out-of-band verification, (4) Indicates whether Forward Secrecy is provided by the negotiated cipher suite.

findings.requirement: The signaling message is sent to the signaling server over TLS. If HTTPS is not used, any on-path attacker can replace the DTLS-SRTP fingerprints and mount a man-in-the-middle attack.

source_files.code_snippet: this.socket = io();

evidence.observation: The signaling channel uses Socket.IO which runs over WebSocket or HTTP long-polling. When deployed on Render.com, this traffic would be encrypted via the platform's HTTPS/WSS termination. However, the Socket.IO server is configured with CORS origin: '*', which means it accepts connections from any origin. This is a significant security weakness because: (1) It allows cross-site request forgery against the signaling service, (2) A malicious site could connect to the signaling server and inject or intercept signaling messages, (3) Combined with the lack of authentication, any website can join rooms and receive SDP offers/answers containing DTLS fingerprints. Furthermore, Socket.IO is initialized with io() (no explicit URL), which will use the page origin, meaning in production it would use WSS, but there is no enforcement mechanism.
evidence.gap_description: CORS origin: '*' on the signaling server allows any website to connect and participate in signaling. This undermines the origin-based security model described in RFC 8827. An attacker on a malicious site could intercept or inject SDP messages containing DTLS fingerprints.

findings.remediation: Restrict the CORS origin to the application's own domain instead of '*'. Add authentication to the signaling channel (e.g., JWT tokens, session cookies) so that only legitimate users can participate in signaling. Verify that Socket.IO connections use WSS in production.

findings.requirement: Calling services are authenticated entities whose origin can be verified via HTTPS. Users must be both connected via HTTPS and able to authenticate with the calling service.

evidence.observation: The application has no user authentication whatsoever. Anyone can join any room by simply knowing (or guessing) the room ID and providing any arbitrary username via sessionStorage. The server does not verify user identity, does not require login credentials, and does not validate that usernames are unique or authentic. The username is stored in sessionStorage on the client side and is self-reported to the server, meaning impersonation is trivial. In the RFC 8827 trust model, the calling service should be able to authenticate users so that meaningful security decisions can be made about who is in a call.
evidence.gap_description: No user authentication mechanism exists. Any user can join any room with any self-chosen username. The server trusts client-provided usernames without verification. This violates the trust model assumption that calling services can authenticate their users.

findings.remediation: Implement user authentication (e.g., login with passwords, OAuth, magic links) before allowing users to join rooms. Validate user identity server-side. Consider implementing room access controls (passwords, invite links with tokens, or host approval for new participants).

findings.requirement: The browser MUST generate the input to the IdP assertion process and display the results of the verification process to the user in a way which cannot be imitated by the calling site. The PeerConnection object should interact with the IdP to provide identity assertions bound to DTLS fingerprints.

evidence.observation: Section 7 of RFC 8827 describes the IdP (Identity Provider) mechanism for peer authentication. The RFC itself notes (Section 7): 'The mechanism described in this section was designed relatively early in the RTCWEB process. In retrospect, the WG was too optimistic about the enthusiasm for this kind of mechanism. At the time of publication, it has not been widely adopted or implemented.' The IdP mechanism has not been widely implemented in any major browser. This feature is not available for application use in practice. The application cannot be expected to implement a mechanism that the browser platforms do not support.

findings.standard_section: 9.1 Communications Security - JS Access to DTLS Keys
findings.requirement: In order to protect against malicious content JavaScript, that JavaScript MUST NOT be allowed to have direct access to -- or perform computations with -- DTLS keys.

evidence.observation: This is a browser implementation requirement. The RTCPeerConnection API does not expose DTLS private keys or allow JavaScript to perform cryptographic operations with them. The application's JavaScript code does not attempt to access DTLS keys. The browser enforces this restriction at the API level.

findings.requirement: Sites SHOULD make users aware of the tradeoffs between privacy (hiding IP addresses via TURN relays) and performance. Anonymous calling services SHOULD instruct the browser to use separate DTLS keys for each call and to use TURN throughout the call. Browsers SHOULD implement the privacy-preserving CNAME generation mode of RFC 7022.

evidence.observation: The application does not inform users about any privacy implications related to IP address exposure. No TURN servers are configured, so there is no option for users to hide their IP addresses from peers. The application creates new RTCPeerConnection instances per peer (which results in new DTLS keys per connection, satisfying the separate keys recommendation by default). Privacy-preserving CNAME generation is a browser implementation detail. While this is not an anonymous calling service, the application still exposes users' IP addresses to all peers without any disclosure or option to prevent it.
evidence.gap_description: No privacy disclosure about IP address exposure to peers. No TURN server support to enable IP privacy mode. No user-facing option to control candidate types.

findings.remediation: Configure TURN server(s) and provide users with an option to use TURN-only mode for IP privacy. Consider adding a privacy notice informing users that their IP address may be visible to other participants.

recommendations.title: Restrict CORS origin and add signaling authentication
recommendations.description: Replace the wildcard CORS origin ('*') on the Socket.IO server with the specific application domain. Implement authentication for the signaling channel (e.g., JWT tokens validated on socket connection) to prevent unauthorized parties from joining rooms or injecting signaling messages. This is the highest-priority fix as it directly undermines the origin-based security model that RFC 8827 relies upon.

recommendations.title: Implement user authentication
recommendations.description: Add a user authentication system (password-based accounts, OAuth/SSO integration, or at minimum room-level passwords) to ensure that participants in a call are identified and authorized. This is essential for the trust model described in RFC 8827, where the calling service is responsible for authenticating users.

recommendations.title: Add HTTPS enforcement at the application level
recommendations.description: Implement HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect in the Express server when running in production. Set the Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header to prevent protocol downgrade attacks. This ensures the origin security model holds even if the hosting platform's configuration changes.

recommendations.title: Configure TURN servers and add IP privacy controls
recommendations.description: Deploy or configure at least one TURN server and add a user-facing option to use TURN-only mode (iceTransportPolicy: 'relay') for IP address privacy. This is required by RFC 8827 Section 6.4 to allow users to hide their IP addresses from peers. Without TURN servers, many NAT traversal scenarios will also fail.

recommendations.title: Implement security characteristics inspector UI
recommendations.description: Add a security inspector UI element that shows: (1) an encryption status indicator (e.g., lock icon) confirming DTLS-SRTP is active, (2) the DTLS cipher suite and SRTP protection profile obtained via RTCPeerConnection.getStats(), (3) whether Forward Secrecy is provided, and (4) the remote peer's DTLS certificate fingerprint for out-of-band verification. This satisfies the MUST requirement in Section 6.5.
